title: Friday Sermon 2 February 2001
description:
Many people assume they have high morals; however these claims need be tested in light of the word of The Quran and Ahadith:

On the day when their tongues and their hands and their feet will bear witness against them as to what they used to do.

On that day will Allah pay them their just due and they will know that Allah alone is the very Perfect Truth that makes things Manifest.

('The Holy Quran'.  Al Noor [The Light]: 25, 26).

The Quranic statement mentioned herein says that on the Day of Judgment the human faculties would bear witness against all  their wrongs committed.

The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be on him) would always answer each Companion's question according to his individual temperament; hence we find varied solutions given to similar inquiries in the Ahadith.

At times individuals refrain from guiding others in the belief that their own morals are not high enough. 

Indeed self-analysis confirms that no singular individual is worthy of checking others, however in the wisdom of the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be on him) noting the good in others, one's own drawbacks, enjoining others to do good makes one aware of one's own deeds and thus begins evident improvement in one's own self:

Abu Saeed al-Khudri narrated (that the Prophet ﷺ) said: When the son of Adam wakes up in the morning, all of his body parts bow to the tongue and say 'Fear Allah regarding us, we are only part of you.  If you are straight we are straight and if you are crooked we are crooked’.

(Abu Isa Muhammad ibn Isa at-Tirmidhi.  'Jami at-Tirmidhi').

The Holy Prophet's (peace and blessings of Allah be on him) admonished against the discord that rumours create:

Sufyan bin Abdullah at-Thaqafi said: I asked ‘O Messenger of Allah!  Inform me about a     matter that I may hold fast to.’  He said 'Say - My Lord is Allah, then be steadfast.’  I said ‘O   Messenger of Allah!  What do you fear most for me?’  So he took hold of his tongue and said 'This.'

(Ibid).

The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be on him) gave the comprehensive counsel to say one's Prayer each time as if it was one's last Prayer.

(Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Yazid ibn Majah.  'Sunan ibn Majah').

The Promised Messiah (on whom be peace) warns against the blight of falsehood.* The Promised Messiah (on whom be peace) said the prayer of ‘Thee alone do we worship and Thee alone do we implore for help’ (al-Fatihah [The Opening]: 5) is a prayer made in the plural rather than the singular, thus one prays for and with the entire mankind, quashing the trait of selfishness.

* Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad - The Promised Messiah & Mahdi.  August 1902.  'Malfoozat: iii'.  145).
